# Transcrição: 02. How to be a good partner

- **Fonte:** 02. How to be a good partner
- **Duração:** 04:59
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Before you get going, we want to remind you both of a few key things that will help make this process a success. First of all, for the partner. Your role is to listen carefully as the storyteller shares each story. You'll need to take detailed notes in the Partner Guide. As you do so, we recommend you circle or underline words, phrases, or ideas that come up more than once. What you're looking for are themes. If a word, a phrase, or an idea comes up more than once, it's a theme. And there's no such thing as a wrong theme. Identifying these themes will be key when it's time to put the why into words. Importantly, be patient and leave the analysis of the themes until after the storytelling. We'll guide you through how to do all that later. In addition to recording themes that emerge from the stories, be sure to pay close attention to body language and tone of voice. Some gestures, for example, talking in a more animated tone, getting choked up, or even going quiet for a moment, all these subtle clues can give context to what's being said and how significant it is for the storyteller. The art of being a great partner is knowing when to ask questions and when to stay quiet. Silence is a key part of this process. It's important that you allow the storyteller to find their own words. It can be awkward to watch someone struggle. Instinctively, you'll want to help. You'll be tempted to put words in their mouth or suggest answers. This is what we sometimes call leading the witness, and we'll actually do more harm than good. Your goal is to create the best possible space for the storyteller to work through the silence and find their own words. So instead of interjecting, write down your questions and circle back once the storyteller has finished their thought. And if you need clarification or want to dig deeper, ask open-ended questions before going back to be a good listener again. Sometimes two people may use different words but mean the same thing. And sometimes two people may say the same word but mean different things. Rather than assume you understand what the storyteller is trying to say based on your definition of the words they're using, ask questions about what they meant by the words they chose. Remember, your goal is to understand the meaning behind what they're saying. Above all, the wide discovery process belongs to them, and they must be allowed to discover who they are rather than who you think they are. So do your very best to be an objective observer. Think of this session as being more like an interview than a two-way exchange. You'll be using your ears way more than your voice. During the process, your storyteller may say something you relate to or perhaps even strongly disagree with. Now is not the time to react to what they say. Keep your focus on the storyteller and how they felt.

## [03:02 - 04:59]

For example, if someone were to say that they were born in Birmingham, England, I may get excited because I'm from near there. I might even be tempted to give a burst of the local Brummie accent. Even though that might be, now is not the time. As Peter mentioned, your storyteller may share something you disagree with. It could be a favorite sports team or an artist or even a religious or political belief. To be an effective partner, withhold your judgment and opinions and stay focused on helping them identify the themes that make them who they are at their natural best. Lastly, it's really important that the person you are helping can really open up and share without filtering or omitting parts of their story. So before you get started, take a moment to reassure them that everything they share will remain confidential. It's an essential commitment to make between the two of you. Maybe even pause the video and make that commitment. It's important. Okay, you now have the basics on being a great partner. If you need a little reminder during the process, you'll find the tips and ideas we just shared at the front of your partner guide. You can also watch the optional video below to see a demo of how the conversation might flow. And now to the person looking for their why, our storyteller. Your job is to be open and honest. Just be yourself. Rather than trying to analyze what you're saying or drive toward what you think your why should be, focus on relaxing into the stories as you tell them. Now take a deep breath and share at least three specific stories, the more the better, openly and honestly. Talk about the people in those stories and the specific place or moment in time. Remember, specifics will get you to where you need to be. This is the time to draw on all that preparation you've done.
